The United States Securities and Exchange Commission’s latest appeal in its case against Ripple doesn’t pose a challenge to the court’s ruling that XRP is not a security.On Oct. 16, the SEC filed a Form C civil appeal with a pre-argument statement against certain aspects of the court’s summary judgment in favor of the defendants in its XRP (XRP) rulings.The appeal, shared by Ripple’s defense attorney James Filan on Oct. 17, asks the court to review its decisions regarding Ripple’s XRP sales on exchanges, as well as personal XRP sales by Ripple CEO Brad Garlinghouse and co-founder Chris Larsen.

In a surprising misstep, the SEC appears to have missed the deadlineto file its appeal, failing to specify its objections to Judge Analisa Torres’ Aug. 7 ruling.After submitting its notice of appeal on Oct. 2, the SEC had a 14-day window to file the requisite Forms C and D by Oct 16.However, the commission failed to meet this critical milestone, leaving its appeal in limbo and potentially dismissed.

Still researching this morning but does anyone know if the SEC filed Form C on their appeal. It was due by COB yesterday (10/16). Form C is a procedural filing that outlines the details of what the party (SEC) intends to specifically appeal regarding the previous Ripple ruling by Judge Torres'.

Stuart Alderoty, Ripple’s Chief Legal Officer, expressed confidence in the company’s position as the case moves forward. “I felt good about our case in the Southern District of New York. I feel even better about our case in the Second Circuit,” Alderoty stated in a recent interview.He believes the SEC’s appeal will ultimately “backfire” and benefit the crypto industry as a whole.The appeals process is set to begin with both parties filing Form C documents, which will outline the specific aspects of Judge Torres’ ruling they intend to challenge. The SEC is due to file its Form C on October 16, 2024, with Ripple following suit seven days later. These filings will provide clarity on the focus of each party’s appeal.

News from Coincheck adding XRP Ripple’s CEO, Brad Garlinghouse, has suggested the possibility of burning some of the XRP held in escrow. This would involve placing the tokens in an unspendable wallet, effectively removing them from circulation. Garlinghouse has shown openness to discussions on this topic, indicating potential adjustments in Ripple’s strategy regarding XRP supply management.
